Winslow Tandler ← Issue 02 Issue 03 →
SECOND ORDER
Sidebar · June 15, 2026
AI, Capital & Work

The 7% Problem

Companies spend about 93 cents of every AI dollar on technology and 7 on the people and process that turn technology into output. That 7 is where the missing returns come from. A research sidebar off "AI spreads while training lags" (Minnesota Star Tribune).

Read the companion deck (PDF)→

THE CLAIM

The training gap is the mechanism behind the returns that have not shown up. The exact 7% that firms decline to spend, on enablement, workflow redesign, and governance, is the complementary investment the productivity literature says has to come first. The firms getting a return are the ones funding that 7%, whatever models they buy.

• 93% of corporate AI budgets go to technology, 7% to people and process (Deloitte; single-source, treat as directional).

• About 95% of enterprise generative-AI pilots produce no measurable profit impact; only 5% reach production with measurable P&L impact (MIT).

• Only about 5.5% of firms see more than 5% of EBIT come from AI; fewer than one in ten see a material enterprise-level effect (McKinsey).

• There are about 499,000 dedicated workplace trainers for 155.5 million jobs: one trainer per 312 workers (BLS; Second Order calculation).

• 44% of employees have used AI against company policy; only 41% work somewhere that has a policy (KPMG).

01 · THE ARITHMETIC

The 7% is where the return comes from

The article reports two facts in separate breaths: 93% of corporate AI budgets go to the technology itself and 7% to training and supporting its use, and "few companies have yet to show a return." The missing return follows from the underfunding. MIT's Project NANDA tracked 30 to 40 billion dollars of enterprise generative-AI spend and found a funnel: about 80% of organizations explore the tools, 20% launch pilots, and 5% reach production with measurable profit impact. McKinsey's 2025 survey lands in the same place from the other direction: only about 39% can link any earnings impact to AI, and only about 5.5% see more than 5% of EBIT come from it (Exhibit 1).

The mechanism is not new. Brynjolfsson, Rock and Syverson named it the productivity J-curve: general-purpose technologies require complementary intangible investment, process redesign, retraining, and reorganization, all expensed rather than capitalized. Measured output dips first while that capital is built, then climbs once it is in place. The 93/7 split is a picture of firms buying the technology and declining to build the intangible capital. The strongest objection is that this is fine and temporary, and that low returns now are the dip before the climb. The J-curve only turns up after that complementary investment is made, and the 93/7 split shows most firms have not made it.

02 · THE CONSTRAINT

Even if every firm tripled the 7%, the training function could not deliver it.

The article's implied fix, more money for training, runs into a wall the piece never mentions: there are almost no trainers. BLS counts about 452,300 training and development specialists and 46,400 managers, roughly 499,000 dedicated workplace trainers, against about 155.5 million jobs. That is one trainer for every 312 workers. The occupation is one of the faster-growing categories BLS tracks, with specialists projected to grow 11% and managers 6% through 2034 against 3% for all occupations. It still adds only about 51,500 people over the decade. Set that against demand: AI use went from near zero to roughly 80% of workers in three years, about 124 million people on current employment (our estimate), or roughly 249 AI users per professional trainer (Exhibit 2).

At one trainer for every 312 workers, a central learning-and-development team cannot deliver the 7% at any plausible headcount. The capacity does not exist and will not exist on any plausible hiring path, so the enablement has to happen in the line organization, the subject of section 04.

03 · THE RELEASE VALVE

Skip the enablement and the AI use moves outside policy

Most workers already use AI whether or not the firm trains them for it. KPMG's 2025 work finds 87% of workers using AI at least weekly and 51% daily, while 44% have used it in ways that break company policy, 58% have relied on AI output without checking it, and only 41% work somewhere that has a generative-AI policy at all. Radha Chavali, the CIO at Affinity Plus, is direct: "Employees are using tools on their own, with sensitive data, because the official training didn't meet their actual needs." Unsanctioned use turns the unspent 7% into a security and compliance exposure, and the published breach-cost premiums for it are large (Exhibit 3).

The companies in the article that show results share one habit, and it has little to do with the size of a training department: they push enablement into the workflow. Affinity Plus rolled out Microsoft Copilot only after organization-wide training on how to use it, when not to use it, and how to verify its output. Thrivent runs peer forums, live demonstrations, office hours, and team-specific sessions, and reports more than 90% of users still active each month and advisers saving about four hours a week. Mayo Clinic pairs fluency courses and hands-on workshops with internal agent-building and a nursing AI competition that drew hundreds of submissions. None is a classroom program run by a central team (Exhibit 4).

The capability-building research says the same thing in the abstract. McKinsey frames it as three layers: AI literacy, then adoption embedded in redesigned roles and incentives, then domain-specific transformation. BCG's 2025 read stresses rigorous experimentation and tracking impact rather than counting seats. The common thread is that the 7% buys a change in how work is done.

SECOND-ORDER EFFECT: THE SKILL PREMIUM MOVES

The article's implied prescription is "spend more on training." The move that works is to stop treating training as a line item and start treating it as an operating-model change, and to change what you hire for. Austen Wilson, quoted in the piece, now screens advisers for being "really relational" with "the heart of a teacher" rather than for technical skill.

As AI absorbs the technical task, the scarce inputs become judgment, verification, and the ability to coach a tool through a team. That reshapes hiring as much as the training budget, and it is the practical reason the capacity gap has to be closed in the line, where managers and peers do the teaching in the course of the work.

WHAT WE'D DO · THE OPERATOR'S PLAYBOOK

1. Re-cut the AI budget to a target ratio. If you are near 93/7, move toward something like 80/20. Once the tool is good enough, another dollar of enablement returns more than another dollar of model upgrade.

2. Train before you deploy, including when not to use the tool. The highest-leverage content is when to avoid the tool and how to verify its output, because 58% of users currently do not check it.

3. Push enablement into the line. At one trainer per 312 people, the model that scales is peer champions, office hours, and manager-led practice, not a central academy you cannot staff.

4. Write the policy before the tool spreads. Only 41% of workers have a generative-AI policy at all, and unsanctioned use fills the gap where none exists.

5. Measure adoption, hours, then P&L. Seats deployed says little on its own. Monthly-active use near 90% and about four hours saved per person are the leading indicators that the spend is working.

6. Hire for judgment and teaching. Screen for the instinct to verify and the ability to coach a team through a tool, not for technical fluency the tool now supplies.

WATCHLIST · WHAT WOULD CHANGE OUR MIND

EBIT among tool-heavy spenders. If the next MIT and McKinsey reads show enterprise earnings impact climbing without a shift in enablement spend, the "returns need the 7%" thesis weakens.

Self-adapting agents. MIT pins the failures on a learning gap. If agentic tools start adapting to workflows on their own, the training requirement falls and the 7% matters less. Track agent adoption against measured returns.

The falsifier. A cohort of firms that bought the tools, did little training, and still booked more than 5% of EBIT from AI. We have not found it. If it shows up at scale, we are wrong.

SCORECARD · CONSENSUS VS. THE TAPE

"AI has no ROI." The tape says AI bought without the 7% has no ROI, while the 5% that integrated it are pulling real money out (MIT).

"More training will fix it." More training is necessary but it cannot run through a central training department, and the unsanctioned use is already happening.

The article's diagnosis. Right, in Soumya Sen's words: firms are "investing in technology but not in figuring out how the technology would be used, who would use it."

SELECTED SOURCES

Minnesota Star Tribune, "AI spreads while training lags" (Hartzog) · Deloitte (93/7 split; CFO Signals / CTO commentary; single-source, finance-framed) · MIT Project NANDA, "The GenAI Divide: State of AI in Business 2025" · McKinsey, "The State of AI" 2025 · Brynjolfsson, Rock and Syverson, "The Productivity J-Curve" (NBER w25148) · KPMG U.S., 2025 ("Shadow AI is already here"; American Worker survey) · BLS OEWS (May 2019, May 2025) and Occupational Outlook Handbook (2024 to 2034 projections, SOC 13-1151, 11-3131); Second Order calculations (data and method in analysis/ai_training_gap/data/) · Carlson Analytics Maturity Model, University of Minnesota.

Estimates are flagged where they appear; single-sourced figures carry a visible caveat. Second Order is provided for discussion purposes only and is not investment, legal, tax, or accounting advice. © 2026 Second Order.
